fields.rationale: The opinion at ¶ 25 expressly endorses the proposition, quoting that "Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion" and affirming dismissal of the extortion claim with prejudice on that basis.

The opinion addresses summary judgment on breach of contract, negligence, fraud, and defamation claims arising from a deed error and never discusses extortion (it only notes coercion is not a known civil cause of action), so it does not support the proposition and the quote check is FABRICATED.

Royal Truck holds that employees authorized to access company information do not exceed authorized access under the CFAA by misusing it, directly supporting that accessing information with valid credentials does not violate the CFAA.

Van Buren holds that one does not exceed authorized access under the CFAA when using valid credentials to obtain information he is entitled to access (reversing the conviction despite an improper purpose), directly supporting the proposition that accessing information with valid credentials does not violate the CFAA.
The opinion states verbatim at ¶ 14 that "Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion," directly and accurately supporting the cited proposition.

The opinion squarely supports the OUTSA-preemption half of the proposition but never addresses any trade-secret-particularity requirement, so the conjunctive proposition overstates what this case holds.

Wilson v. Collins is a constitutional challenge to Ohio's DNA collection statute (Fourth/Fifth Amendment and due process) and says nothing about HIPAA or any HIPAA private right of action, so it addresses a completely different topic and does not support the proposition.

The Sixth Circuit in Stolle expressly held that the OUTSA displaces overlapping common-law tort claims (tortious interference and conspiracy) that merely restate the operative facts of a trade-secret-misappropriation claim, directly supporting the proposition.

Iqbal expressly states that to survive a motion to dismiss a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter, accepted as true, to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face, directly and accurately supporting the proposition.

Iqbal directly holds that the assumption of truth is inapplicable to legal conclusions and that courts are not bound to accept a legal conclusion couched as a factual allegation, squarely supporting the proposition.

Twombly established the plausibility pleading standard, holding that a complaint must plead enough facts to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face, directly and accurately supporting the proposition.

The opinion expressly holds at ¶ 25, citing Angelini, that Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion and affirms dismissal of the extortion claim with prejudice, directly supporting the proposition.

The opinion holds at ¶ 14 that "Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion," supporting the core point, but as a single Third District decision it does not itself show Ohio courts have "repeatedly" so held, mildly overstating the rule.

The opinion holds only that "coercion" is not a cognizable civil cause of action (¶ 26); it never addresses or mentions extortion, so it does not support the proposition that Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion.

Hecht v. Levin holds that a statement made in a judicial proceeding enjoys an absolute privilege against a defamation action as long as it bears some reasonable relation to the proceeding, directly supporting the proposition.

Surace v. Wuliger holds that defamatory statements made in judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged where they bear a reasonable relation to the proceeding, directly supporting the proposition that communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged.

Kenty addresses only tortious interference with a contract and civil conspiracy regarding insurance premiums on a loan; it says nothing about extortion, good-faith basis for demands, or any no-legitimate-claim element, so it does not support the proposition.

Pendergraft supports the general idea that a bad-faith threat to litigate (even with false affidavits) is not Hobbs Act extortion, but it does not turn on the cited frame of legitimate claims or a damages element, the proposition overstates its scope, and the FABRICATED quote check requires at least a Yellow downgrade.

Kenty addresses tortious interference with a contract and civil conspiracy in an insurance-overcharge dispute and says nothing about extortion, settlement demands, or privilege for demands based on enforceable legal rights, and the quote check is FABRICATED.

Jackson holds a reputation-injury threat is extortion under 18 U.S.C. 875(d) when the threatener has no plausible claim of right (here, threatening tabloid disclosure of paternity to extort money from Cosby); it does not support the proposition that informing a party you will report misconduct to authorities is lawful, and the supporting quote is fabricated.

Flatley held the opposite: a monetary settlement demand coupled with threats to report misconduct to authorities and publicize allegations constituted criminal extortion as a matter of law, and the quoted language is fabricated.

Pendergraft holds that a bad-faith threat to sue a government, even with fabricated affidavits, is not wrongful under the Hobbs Act; it does not address advising a party of consequences from lawful reporting to authorities, so it only partially fits the proposition, and the quote check is FABRICATED.

Kulch supports that Ohio public policy protects an at-will employee's reporting of workplace violations to a regulator (OSHA) via a wrongful-discharge tort, but the proposition overstates and generalizes the holding (e.g., adds reporting to "sponsors," which the case never addresses, and omits the statute's strict-compliance limits).

Flatley holds the opposite of the proposition: it found that Mauro's threats to expose/report Flatley's alleged misconduct unless paid constituted criminal extortion as a matter of law, and that such threats are extortionate even when the victim actually committed the crime or indiscretion.

The opinion addresses only OUTSA preemption of common-law claims under the Allied same-facts standard and never discusses a trade-secret particularity/identification requirement, so it does not support the proposition (and the quote check was FABRICATED).

Jackson holds the opposite: under 18 U.S.C. 875(d) the truth of the damaging allegations is not a defense, and even a truthful threatened disclosure is extortion when it lacks a nexus to a plausible claim of right, so the case does not support and contradicts the proposition that truthful disclosure of misconduct cannot be extortion.

This opinion concerns the constitutionality of Ohio's DNA collection statute under the Fourth, Fifth, and Fourteenth Amendments and never mentions HIPAA or any private right of action under it, so it does not support the proposition.

Stolle expressly holds that the OUTSA displaces overlapping common-law tort claims (tortious interference and conspiracy) that merely restate the same operative facts as the trade-secret-misappropriation claim, directly supporting the cited proposition.

Van Buren holds that one exceeds authorized access only by obtaining information from areas of a computer that are off-limits, not by misusing information accessible with valid credentials, directly and accurately supporting the proposition.

The Sixth Circuit in Royal Truck directly held that an employee authorized to access company information does not exceed authorized access under the CFAA by misusing it, expressly adopting the narrow rule and aligning with the Second, Fourth, and Ninth Circuits.

Hecht v. Levin holds litigation-related defamatory statements are absolutely privileged only when they bear some reasonable relation to the proceeding, so the unqualified "any defamation-based claim" framing overstates the holding by omitting that relevance limitation.

The opinion supports OUTSA preemption of tortious-interference and conversion/unjust-enrichment theories only under a partial 'same facts' standard, preempting just the portions resting on misappropriation while expressly preserving claims with an independent factual basis, so the proposition's categorical framing overstates the qualified holding.

fields.brief_block: A complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face. Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009) (internal quotation marks omitted); Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007).
fields.opinion_block: To survive a motion to dismiss, a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter, accepted as true, to "state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face."
fields.finding_analysis: Iqbal states this standard nearly verbatim at page 678, the cited pincite, holding that to survive a motion to dismiss a complaint must contain sufficient factual matter, accepted as true, to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face. The proposition accurately tracks the opinion's language and holding.

fields.brief_block: Courts disregard legal conclusions couched as factual allegations. Iqbal, 556 U.S. at 678.
fields.opinion_block: the tenet that a court must accept as true all of the allegations contained in a complaint is inapplicable to legal conclusions. Threadbare recitals of the elements of a cause of action, supported by mere conclusory statements, do not suffice.
fields.finding_analysis: At page 678, Iqbal holds that the assumption of truth does not extend to legal conclusions and that a court is "not bound to accept as true a legal conclusion couched as a factual allegation," which directly supports the brief's proposition that courts disregard legal conclusions couched as factual allegations.

fields.brief_block: A complaint must contain sufficient factual matter to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face. Ashcroft v. Iqbal, 556 U.S. 662, 678 (2009) (internal quotation marks omitted); Bell Atl. Corp. v. Twombly, 550 U.S. 544, 570 (2007).
fields.finding_analysis: Twombly is the source of the "plausible on its face" pleading standard the brief invokes, and the cited pincite is exact: at page 570 the Court holds that it requires "only enough facts to state a claim to relief that is plausible on its face," adding that "[b]ecause the plaintiffs here have not nudged their claims across the line from conceivable to plausible, their complaint must be dismissed." The brief's "sufficient factual matter" phrasing tracks the companion Iqbal formulation it cites alongside Twombly, but the substantive proposition is squarely supported by Twombly at the page cited.

fields.brief_block: Ohio does not recognize a civil cause of action for “extortion.” See Simpson v. Voiture Nationale La Société des Quarante Hommes et Huit Chevaux, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15 (2d Dist.) (affirming dismissal; “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion”), citing First Fed. Bank of Ohio v. Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6 (3d Dist.) [...]
fields.opinion_block: Next, we consider the sanctions for the counterclaim asserted by appellant for extortion. Simply put, Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion. Thus, the counterclaim was based upon a legal nullity.
fields.finding_analysis: Angelini directly supports the proposition: the court holds that “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion” and treats the extortion counterclaim as “a legal nullity.” The quoted language is verbatim. One caveat for citation form: the supporting holding appears at ¶ 14, not at ¶ 6 as the brief pincites; ¶ 6 recounts the jury verdict and the 2009 mistrial and says nothing about extortion.

fields.brief_block: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion. Simpson, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15; Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6; Heskett, 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26.
fields.opinion_block: Simply put, Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion. Thus, the counterclaim was based upon a legal nullity.
fields.finding_analysis: Angelini supports the proposition that Ohio recognizes no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion: the court flatly states that “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion” and affirms sanctions because the extortion counterclaim “was based upon a legal nullity.” The brief pincites ¶ 6, but the extortion holding is at ¶ 14; ¶ 6 concerns the jury verdict and mistrial, not extortion.

fields.brief_block: To the extent Kettering reframes its grievance as defamation or abuse-of-process, communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged. Hecht v. Levin, 66 Ohio St. 3d 458, 460-61, 613 N.E.2d 585 (1993) [...]
fields.finding_analysis: Hecht v. Levin directly supports this proposition. The court held that "[a] statement made in a judicial proceeding enjoys an absolute privilege against a defamation action as long as the allegedly defamatory statement is reasonably related to the proceeding in which it appears" (¶7), and its syllabus and holding restate that a statement enjoys absolute privilege "as long as the statement bears some reasonable relation to the proceeding" (¶22). The brief's formulation — communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged — tracks the holding faithfully, including the reasonable-relation qualifier.

fields.brief_block: Any defamation-based claim arising from litigation conduct is absolutely privileged. Hecht, 66 Ohio St. 3d at 460-61.
fields.opinion_block: A statement made in a judicial proceeding enjoys an absolute privilege against a defamation action as long as the allegedly defamatory statement is reasonably related to the proceeding in which it appears.
fields.finding_analysis: Hecht supports an absolute litigation privilege for defamation claims, but the brief overstates its reach by dropping the court's express qualifier. The court conditioned the privilege on the statement bearing "some reasonable relation to the proceeding" and emphasized that "[t]he sole proviso was that the statement must bear 'some reasonable relation to the judicial proceeding in which it appears'" (¶7; see also ¶22). The brief's unqualified assertion that "any" defamation-based claim "arising from litigation conduct is absolutely privileged" removes that reasonable-relation requirement, sweeping in litigation-adjacent statements the court did not hold to be immune. The cited principle is present and on point, but the proposition is broader than what Hecht actually decided.

fields.brief_block: Ohio does not recognize a civil cause of action for “extortion.” See Simpson v. Voiture Nationale La Société des Quarante Hommes et Huit Chevaux, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15 (2d Dist.) (affirming dismissal; “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion”), citing First Fed. Bank of Ohio v. Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6 (3d Dist.), and Heskett v. Van Horn Title Agency, Inc., 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26 (10th Dist.).
fields.opinion_block: Neither are we aware of "coercion" as a cognizable civil cause of action. Cf. R.C. 2905.12 (defining the crime of "coercion," a misdemeanor of the second degree).
fields.finding_analysis:
Heskett is a summary-judgment appeal in a title/mortgage dispute, and the cited paragraph (¶ 26) addresses "coercion," not "extortion." The court states it is "not aware of 'coercion' as a cognizable civil cause of action" and cross-references R.C. 2905.12, the criminal coercion statute. The word "extortion" appears nowhere in the opinion.

Coercion and extortion are sibling offenses within the same statutory chapter (R.C. Chapter 2905), so Heskett offers analogous support for the broader idea that a criminal compulsion offense does not create a civil cause of action. But the brief attaches Heskett to an extortion-specific proposition the opinion never decided, so the support is partial rather than direct. Note also that the quoted language "Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion" is attributed in the brief to Simpson, not Heskett, and does not appear in this opinion.

fields.brief_block: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion. Simpson, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15; Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6; Heskett, 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26.
fields.opinion_block: Neither are we aware of "coercion" as a cognizable civil cause of action. Cf. R.C. 2905.12 (defining the crime of "coercion," a misdemeanor of the second degree).
fields.finding_analysis:
The brief cites Heskett ¶ 26 for the proposition that Ohio courts have "repeatedly held" there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion, but ¶ 26 addresses coercion, not extortion, and the opinion never uses the word "extortion." The court's actual statement is a single, somewhat tentative observation that it is "not aware of 'coercion' as a cognizable civil cause of action," cross-referencing the criminal coercion statute (R.C. 2905.12) — not a repeated or settled holding about extortion.

Because coercion and extortion are closely related offenses in the same statutory chapter, Heskett lends analogous support to the general principle, but it does not establish an extortion-specific rule and is one intermediate-appellate aside rather than evidence of courts "repeatedly" so holding. The support is therefore partial.

fields.brief_block: No Legitimate Claim or Privilege — demands made without a good-faith basis are unprotected. Kenty v. Transamerica Premium Ins. Co., 72 Ohio St.3d 415, 419 (1995).
fields.finding_analysis:
Kenty v. Transamerica Premium Ins. Co. is an Ohio Supreme Court tort case about whether the tort of tortious interference with a contractual relationship is recognized in Ohio (the court holds that it is) and the elements required to prove that tort and civil conspiracy. It does not address extortion, the "no legitimate claim or privilege" element, or whether demands made without a good-faith basis are protected or unprotected.

The opinion does list "lack of justification" as the fourth element of tortious interference with a contract, but that is a distinct doctrine concerning a third party's interference with an existing contract; it says nothing about the legitimacy of a claimant's own demands and supplies no support for the brief's extortion proposition.

fields.brief_block: “Where claims are based on enforceable legal rights, settlement demands are privileged.” — Kenty, 72 Ohio St.3d at 419.
fields.finding_analysis:
Kenty v. Transamerica Premium Ins. Co. is an Ohio Supreme Court tort case about whether tortious interference with a contractual relationship is recognized in Ohio and the elements of that tort and of civil conspiracy. It does not address settlement demands, the privilege attaching to demands based on enforceable legal rights, or extortion.

The sentence offered as a direct quotation — "Where claims are based on enforceable legal rights, settlement demands are privileged" — appears nowhere in the opinion; it is fabricated. Neither the quoted language nor the principle it states is found in or supported by this decision.

fields.brief_block: Reporting misconduct to regulators and sponsors is protected conduct: [...] Ohio's public policy protections (Kulch v. Structural Fibers, Inc., 78 Ohio St.3d 134, 150 (1997)).
fields.opinion_block: Retaliation against employees who file OSHA complaints concerning unsafe or unhealthy conditions in the workplace is an absolute affront to Ohio's public policy favoring workplace safety. Such retaliation clearly contravenes the public policy of this state.
fields.finding_analysis:
Kulch supports the "regulators" half of this proposition. The Ohio Supreme Court held that an at-will employee discharged for reporting workplace-safety violations to OSHA may maintain a common-law tort action for wrongful discharge in violation of Ohio public policy, recognizing a Greeley-based public-policy exception to employment-at-will. To that extent, the case does stand for the idea that reporting misconduct to a government regulator is protected conduct under Ohio law.

The overstatement is twofold. First, Kulch's protection is anchored in reporting to a public regulatory agency (OSHA) about workplace safety and in Ohio's Whistleblower Statute, R.C. 4113.52; the opinion never addresses reporting to private "sponsors," so the brief extends the holding beyond what the case decided by folding sponsors into it. Second, the case's reach is narrower than the brief's generalized "reporting of misconduct" framing -- it is confined to safety/whistleblower reporting to regulators. The proposition is therefore accurate as to regulators but unsupported as to sponsors.

fields.brief_block: Further Kettering fails to identify any trade secret with particularity or reasonable secrecy measures. [...] Office Depot, Inc. v. Impact Off. Prods., LLC, 821 F. Supp. 2d 912, 919-23 (N.D. Ohio 2011) (particularity and OUTSA preemption).
fields.opinion_block: The sole ground asserted for dismissal is that these claims are displaced and preempted by the Ohio Uniform Trade Secret Act (“OUTSA”).
fields.finding_analysis:
Office Depot squarely supports the OUTSA-preemption half of this parenthetical: it is the opinion's central holding that Office Depot's common-law claims are preempted by O.R.C. § 1333.67(A) to the extent they restate misappropriation-of-trade-secret facts, surviving only where an independent factual basis exists.

The parenthetical's other half -- "particularity" -- finds no footing. The motion was decided on a single ground: OUTSA preemption. The opinion never addresses whether a trade secret must be identified with particularity, nor reasonable secrecy measures as a legal requirement (its references to Office Depot's confidentiality policies are factual background only). Citing this case for a particularity requirement therefore overstates what it holds.

fields.brief_block: The Complaint identifies only broad categories of information “clinical data” and “research processes”, which do not satisfy the particularity requirement. Office Depot, 821 F. Supp. 2d at 919-23.
fields.finding_analysis:
Office Depot, Inc. v. Impact Office Products, LLC is a Rule 12(b)(6) motion-to-dismiss decision about whether Office Depot's common-law claims were preempted by the Ohio Uniform Trade Secrets Act. It does not address the trade-secret particularity pleading requirement, nor whether identifying only broad categories of information defeats such a requirement -- the decision turns entirely on OUTSA preemption.

The quoted phrases "clinical data" and "research processes" do not appear anywhere in this opinion; they are the Kettering complaint's own terms, not language from the cited case.

fields.brief_block: Its tortious-interference and conversion theories are preempted by OUTSA where the alleged harm stems from misappropriation. Ohio Rev. Code § 1333.67(A); Office Depot, 821 F. Supp. 2d at 918-23.
fields.opinion_block: The majority interpretation is that common-law claims are preempted only to the extent that they are based on misappropriation-of-trade-secrets facts.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion directly supports this proposition. It holds that Office Depot's tortious-interference counts (Counts Three and Six) are preempted by OUTSA to the extent they rest on misappropriation-of-trade-secret facts, surviving only on an "independent factual basis," and it endorses the same partial-preemption rule for conversion (citing Powell Products, preempting conversion "to extent it relied on misappropriation-of-trade-secret allegations," and Hauck, preempting conversion predicated on misappropriation). The brief's qualifier -- preemption "where the alleged harm stems from misappropriation" -- accurately tracks the opinion's conditional, same-facts standard.

fields.brief_block: The alleged Computer Fraud and Abuse Act claim is foreclosed by Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374, 380-86 (2021) and Royal Truck & Trailer Sales & Serv., Inc. v. Kraft, 974 F.3d 756, 758-61 (6th Cir. 2020), because Defendant Collier had accessed the information with valid credentials and at Kettering's direction during a system outage.
fields.opinion_block: But because Royal concedes that the employees were authorized to access the information in question, it has failed to satisfy the statutory requirements for stating a claim under the CFAA.
fields.finding_analysis: Royal Truck directly supports the cited legal principle. The Sixth Circuit held that because the defendant employees were authorized to access the company information at issue, they did not "exceed" their "authorized access" under § 1030(a)(2) even though they later misused that information in violation of company policy, and on that basis it affirmed dismissal of the CFAA claim. The proposition that accessing information with valid credentials does not violate the CFAA is the opinion's central holding. Whether it actually forecloses Kettering's claim turns on the brief's factual premise that Collier accessed the data with valid credentials, which this opinion cannot itself confirm, but the legal rule the case is cited for is accurately stated.

fields.brief_block: The Sixth Circuit applies the same rule. Royal Truck, 974 F.3d at 758-61.
fields.opinion_block: As we do today, the Second, Fourth, and Ninth Circuits have also held that one who is authorized to access a computer does not exceed her authorized access by violating an employer's restrictions on the use of information once it is validly accessed.
fields.finding_analysis: Supported. Royal Truck is a Sixth Circuit decision that adopts the narrow reading of "exceeds authorized access," holding that an employee with authorization to access company information does not exceed that authorization by misusing the information in violation of company policy. The court expressly aligned the Sixth Circuit with the Second, Fourth, and Ninth Circuits on this point, which accurately supports the brief's assertion that the Sixth Circuit applies the same narrow rule.

fields.brief_block: Ohio does not recognize a civil cause of action for “extortion.” See Simpson v. Voiture Nationale La Société des Quarante Hommes et Huit Chevaux, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15 (2d Dist.) (affirming dismissal; “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion”), citing First Fed. Bank of Ohio v. Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6 (3d Dist.), and Heskett v. Van Horn Title Agency, Inc., 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26 (10th Dist.).
fields.opinion_block: The trial court also cited First Fed. Bank of Ohio v. Angelini, 3d Dist. Crawford No. 3-11-16, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6, which states, “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion[,]” [...] Based upon the cited cases, we agree with the trial court’s decision to dismiss these claims with prejudice.
fields.finding_analysis: Simpson supports the proposition. At ¶ 25 the court affirms dismissal of the extortion claim with prejudice, expressly adopting Angelini's statement that “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion,” and the quoted language is reproduced verbatim and accurately attributed. One caution worth flagging: the supporting discussion sits at ¶ 25, not ¶¶ 13-15 as cited — ¶¶ 13-15 address the unrelated defamation claim — so the pinpoint is off even though the substance holds.

fields.brief_block: Ohio courts have repeatedly held there is no stand-alone civil cause of action for extortion. Simpson, 2021-Ohio-2131, ¶¶ 13-15; Angelini, 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6; Heskett, 2006-Ohio-6900, ¶ 26.
fields.opinion_block: R.C. 2905.11 and R.C. 2905.12 are criminal statutes prohibiting extortion and coercion, respectively. [...] The trial court also cited First Fed. Bank of Ohio v. Angelini [...] 2012-Ohio-2136, ¶ 6, which states, “Ohio law does not recognize a civil action for extortion[,]” [...] Based upon the cited cases, we agree with the trial court’s decision to dismiss these claims with prejudice.
fields.finding_analysis: Simpson supports the proposition. At ¶ 25 the court holds that R.C. 2905.11 (extortion) is a criminal statute that creates no private cause of action and affirms dismissal with prejudice, relying on both Angelini and Heskett — the opinion itself stacking prior authority, which bears out the brief's “repeatedly held” framing (the “stand-alone” phrasing also tracks the trial court's language quoted at ¶ 5). As with the parallel citation, the relevant holding is at ¶ 25, not the cited ¶¶ 13-15, which concern the defamation claim.

fields.brief_block: Overlapping torts are displaced by the Ohio Uniform Trade Secrets Act. See Ohio Rev. Code § 1333.67(A); Stolle Mach. Co. v. RAM Precision Indus., 605 F. App'x 473, 484 (6th Cir. 2015).
fields.opinion_block: The OUTSA incorporates the Uniform Trade Secrets Act's displacement of "conflicting tort, restitutionary, and other laws of this state providing civil remedies for misappropriation of a trade secret." Ohio Rev.Code § 1333.67(A).
fields.finding_analysis: Section V of the opinion (beginning at the cited page 484) squarely supports this proposition: the Sixth Circuit held that the OUTSA displaces overlapping common-law tort claims, affirming dismissal of Stolle's tortious-interference and conspiracy claims as displaced because they merely restated the operative facts of its trade-secret-misappropriation claim. The pincite to page 484 is accurate.

fields.brief_block: Moreover, to the extent Kettering asserts overlapping torts based on the same nucleus of facts, the Ohio Uniform Trade Secrets Act displaces those claims—Ohio Rev. Code § 1333.67(A); Stolle Mach., 605 F. App'x at 484.
fields.opinion_block: The test to determine whether a state law claim is displaced by OUTSA is to determine whether "the claims are no more than a restatement of the same operative facts" that formed the basis of the plaintiff's statutory claim for trade secret misappropriation.
fields.finding_analysis: The opinion supports this proposition, including the "same nucleus of facts" qualifier: the court adopted the test that an overlapping tort claim is displaced by the OUTSA when it is "no more than a restatement of the same operative facts" underlying the trade-secret-misappropriation claim, and applied it to displace Stolle's tortious-interference and conspiracy claims. The brief's "same nucleus of facts" tracks the opinion's "same operative facts" standard, and the page 484 pincite is accurate.

fields.brief_block: To the extent Kettering reframes its grievance as defamation or abuse-of-process, communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged. [...] Surace v. Wilier, 25 Ohio St. 3d 229, 232-33, 495 N.E.2d 939 (1986).
fields.opinion_block: Thus, we hold that as a matter of public policy, under the doctrine of absolute privilege in a judicial proceeding, a claim alleging that a defamatory statement was made in a written pleading does not state a cause of action where the allegedly defamatory statement bears some reasonable relation to the judicial proceeding in which it appears.
fields.finding_analysis: Surace v. Wuliger directly supports the proposition. The opinion adopts and applies the doctrine of absolute privilege in judicial proceedings, holding that a defamatory statement made in a pleading is absolutely privileged where it "bears some reasonable relation to the judicial proceeding in which it appears." The brief's formulation -- that communications made in or reasonably related to judicial proceedings are absolutely privileged -- tracks the opinion's "reasonable relation" standard, and the pincite (232-33) falls squarely within the court's analysis adopting that rule.

fields.brief_block: “Informing a party that you will report their misconduct to appropriate authorities if corrective action is not taken does not constitute an unlawful threat.” United States v. Jackson, 180 F.3d 55, 70 (2d Cir. 1999). Thus, advising Kettering of Ms. Collier's statutory obligations cannot constitute extortion.
fields.opinion_block: [N]ot all threats to engage in speech that will have the effect of damaging another person’s reputation, even if a forbearance from speaking is conditioned on the payment of money, are wrongful. For example, the purchaser of an allegedly defective product may threaten to complain to a consumer protection agency or to bring suit in a public forum if the manufacturer does not make good on its warranty.... We doubt that Congress intended § 875(d) to criminalize acts such as these.
fields.finding_analysis:
The quoted sentence does not appear anywhere in Jackson; it is a fabricated quotation, not the court's language. Substantively, the case partially supports the underlying idea. Jackson does say that not every threat to make a reputation-damaging disclosure is wrongful, and it offers illustrations — threatening to complain to a consumer protection agency or bring suit — that resemble the brief's "report misconduct to authorities" scenario.

But the brief overstates by presenting this as a categorical rule. Jackson's holding is the opposite of categorical: a threat to reputation is lawful only where the threatener has, or reasonably believes she has, a "plausible claim of right" to the money sought and the threat bears a "nexus" to that claim; where it does not, the threat is "inherently wrongful" and violates § 875(d). The brief drops that claim-of-right qualification, which is the heart of the decision.

fields.brief_block: Courts consistently hold that truthful disclosure of misconduct does not constitute extortion. Flatley, 39 Cal.4th at 330; Jackson, 180 F.3d at 70.
fields.opinion_block: [W]hen a threat is made to injure the reputation of another, the truth of the damaging allegations underlying the threat is not a defense to a charge of extortion under § 875(d).
fields.finding_analysis:
Jackson holds the opposite of the proposition. The brief cites it for the rule that truthful disclosure of misconduct does not constitute extortion, but the Second Circuit states the established principle that truth is not a defense: "when a threat is made to injure the reputation of another, the truth of the damaging allegations underlying the threat is not a defense to a charge of extortion under § 875(d)." Discussing Pignatelli, the court reinforces this — a person is free to publish true facts (at the risk of a libel suit), but is "not free to threaten to injure their reputations ... in order to settle his claim," and it "made no difference" whether the threatener's underlying assertion was correct.

Jackson thus distinguishes lawful from unlawful threats by reference to a plausible claim of right and nexus — not by whether the disclosed information is true. Citing it for the categorical proposition that truthful disclosure cannot be extortion misattributes a holding to the case that contradicts what it actually decided.

fields.brief_block: Mere “hard bargaining,” even when the consequences are severe, is not extortion where claims are legitimate and conduct threatened is lawful. See United States v. Pendergraft, 297 F.3d 1198, 1205 (11th Cir. 2002).
fields.opinion_block: To show a wrongful objective, the Government must show that Pendergraft and Spielvogel had no lawful claim to the money they sought. ... A threat to litigate, by itself, is not necessarily "wrongful" within the meaning of the Hobbs Act.
fields.finding_analysis:
Pendergraft does support the underlying Hobbs Act principle the brief invokes: to be extortion, conduct must be "wrongful," which requires both a wrongful objective (no lawful claim to the money sought) and a wrongful means, and the court holds that resorting to lawful process — there, a threat to litigate — is not inherently wrongful. To that extent the proposition that pursuing legitimate claims through lawful conduct is not extortion is fairly drawn from the opinion.

The brief overstates in two ways. First, the quoted phrase "hard bargaining," appears nowhere in the opinion; Pendergraft frames its analysis around threats to litigate, not commercial "hard bargaining," so the quotation marks misattribute language the court never used. Second, the holding is expressly narrow — the court limits it to a "threat to file litigation against Marion County, even if made in bad faith and supported by false affidavits" — so citing it for a broad, general "hard bargaining is not extortion" rule extends the case beyond what it decided.

fields.brief_block: “Advising of possible consequences from lawful reporting cannot form the basis of an extortion claim.” — United States v. Pendergraft, 297 F.3d at 1205.
fields.opinion_block: Nevertheless, our holding is a narrow one. We hold that Pendergraft and Spielvogel's threat to file litigation against Marion County, even if made in bad faith and supported by false affidavits, was not "wrongful" within the meaning of the Hobbs Act.
fields.finding_analysis:
United States v. Pendergraft is a Hobbs Act criminal-extortion case about whether a threat to file a lawsuit against a county government — even one made in bad faith and backed by false affidavits — is "wrongful." It does not address advising a party of the possible consequences of lawful reporting.

The sentence the brief places in quotation marks appears nowhere in the opinion, and neither does the principle it states. The opinion never discusses "reporting" or "advising of consequences"; its reasoning is confined to threats to litigate. The brief thus attributes a specific, verbatim-quoted rule to a case that does not contain it. While both concern what is not extortion, the reporting principle is a distinct doctrine the court did not reach, and the case's own narrow holding (quoted above) shows it was deciding only the litigation-threat question.

fields.brief_block: The alleged Computer Fraud and Abuse Act claim is foreclosed by Van Buren v. United States, 593 U.S. 374, 380-86 (2021) and Royal Truck & Trailer Sales & Serv., Inc. v. Kraft, 974 F.3d 756, 758-61 (6th Cir. 2020), because Defendant Collier had accessed the information with valid credentials and at Kettering's direction during a system outage.
fields.opinion_block: an individual “exceeds authorized access” when he accesses a computer with authorization but then obtains information located in particular areas of the computer—such as files, folders, or databases—that are off limits to him.
fields.finding_analysis:
Van Buren supports the core of the foreclosure argument: the Court held that a person who accesses a computer with authorization and obtains information "otherwise available to [him]" does not violate the CFAA's "exceeds authorized access" clause even when his purpose is improper, which is the principle the brief invokes for Collier's authorized, directed access.

The proposition overstates the holding, however, by reducing the test to mere possession of "valid credentials." Van Buren's rule turns on whether the accesser was entitled to obtain the particular information, not on credentials as such: the Court expressly states that a person who uses authorized access to reach a prohibited "Folder X," to which he lacks access, "violates the CFAA by obtaining such information." Valid login credentials therefore do not categorically defeat a CFAA claim, so the categorical phrasing in the brief sweeps somewhat broader than what the case decided.

fields.brief_block: The Supreme Court construes “exceeds authorized access” narrowly to cover obtaining information from particular areas of a computer—such as files, folders, or databases—to which a person lacks access privileges, not misuse of information obtained with authorized credentials. Van Buren, 593 U.S. at 380-86.
fields.finding_analysis: This accurately states Van Buren's holding. The Court held that "an individual 'exceeds authorized access' when he accesses a computer with authorization but then obtains information located in particular areas of the computer—such as files, folders, or databases—that are off limits to him," and that the clause "does not cover those who, like Van Buren, have improper motives for obtaining information that is otherwise available to them" — precisely the area-based, not misuse-based, construction the brief describes, and the quoted phrase "exceeds authorized access" appears verbatim throughout the opinion.

fields.brief_block: HIPAA provides no private right of action, and the materials at issue are de-identified by subject codes, not “PHI.” See Wilson v. Collins, 517 F.3d 421, 429 (6th Cir. 2008); 45 C.F.R. § 164.514(b)(2).
fields.finding_analysis:
Wilson v. Collins is a Sixth Circuit constitutional-law case about a prisoner's challenge to Ohio's DNA-collection statute under the Fourth Amendment, due process, and equal protection. It does not address HIPAA, protected health information, or any HIPAA private right of action.

The opinion never mentions HIPAA. It does discuss a "private right of action," but only as to Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 — holding (per Alexander v. Sandoval) that § 602 regulations cannot be enforced through a private cause of action — an entirely different statute from the one the brief invokes. The cited pincite, page 429, falls in the substantive-due-process discussion of whether a felon has a fundamental privacy interest in his DNA profile; it says nothing about private rights of action of any kind. The citation does not support the HIPAA proposition.

fields.brief_block: HIPAA does not create a private right of action. Wilson, 517 F.3d at 429.
fields.finding_analysis:
Wilson v. Collins is a Sixth Circuit constitutional-law case about a prisoner's challenge to Ohio's DNA-collection statute under the Fourth Amendment, due process, and equal protection. It does not address HIPAA or whether HIPAA creates a private right of action.

HIPAA appears nowhere in the opinion. The only "private right of action" analysis concerns Title VI of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 (Alexander v. Sandoval), a different statute reached on a different theory. The cited page 429 is part of the substantive-due-process discussion of a felon's claimed privacy interest in his DNA information and contains no private-right-of-action holding at all. The case cannot support the proposition for which it is cited.
